description: Cultural Medical Knowledge Base for Voices of Home. Each entry maps a cultural symptom expression to clinical significance. Sources from published medical anthropology literature.

entries.clinical_mapping: Deep bone or joint pain, often associated with sickle cell vaso-occlusive crisis in Haitian populations. May also indicate severe arthritis or osteomyelitis.
entries.source_citation: Farmer, P. (2006). AIDS and Accusation: Haiti and the Geography of Blame. University of California Press; Desrosiers & Bhatt, J Immigr Minor Health, 2008.

entries.clinical_mapping: Culturally understood as blood impurity causing skin conditions, fatigue, and general malaise. May correspond to anemia, dermatological conditions, or systemic infection.
entries.source_citation: Brodwin, P. (1996). Medicine and Morality in Haiti. Cambridge University Press.

entries.clinical_mapping: Blood that has moved to the head, causing headaches, dizziness, blurred vision. Often corresponds to hypertensive crisis or severe migraine.
entries.source_citation: DeSantis & Thomas, J Transcult Nurs, 1992; Colin & Paperwalla, Transcultural Medicine, 2003.

entries.literal_translation: fright / soul loss from a shocking event
entries.clinical_mapping: Culture-bound syndrome presenting as anxiety, insomnia, appetite loss, and social withdrawal following a traumatic event. Clinically maps to acute stress disorder, PTSD, or generalized anxiety disorder.
entries.source_citation: Rubel, A.J. (1964). The epidemiology of a folk illness: susto in Hispanic America. Ethnology, 3(3); DSM-5 Cultural Formulation, APA 2013.

entries.literal_translation: blocked stomach / food stuck to intestinal wall
entries.clinical_mapping: Believed to be food adhering to the stomach or intestinal wall. Presents as bloating, nausea, diarrhea, loss of appetite. Clinically maps to gastroenteritis, functional dyspepsia, or constipation.
entries.source_citation: Weller et al., Medical Anthropology, 1993; Zacharias, S., Hispanic Health Care International, 2006.

entries.clinical_mapping: Illness believed to be caused by envious gaze, especially in children. Presents as fever, irritability, crying, vomiting, diarrhea in infants. Clinically evaluate for febrile illness, viral infection, or dehydration.
entries.source_citation: Baer & Bustillo, Medical Anthropology Quarterly, 1993; Pachter, L.M., Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med, 1994.

entries.clinical_mapping: Broad idiom of distress describing emotional suffering, headaches, trembling, stomach upset, and difficulty concentrating. Can map to anxiety disorders, depression, somatic symptom disorder, or adjustment disorder.
entries.source_citation: Guarnaccia, P.J. et al., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1989; Lewis-Fernandez et al., DSM-5 Cultural Concepts of Distress.

entries.clinical_mapping: Acute episode of emotional distress with uncontrollable crying, trembling, shouting, dissociative episodes, and sometimes fainting. Often triggered by grief or family conflict. Clinically maps to panic attack, dissociative episode, or acute stress reaction.
entries.source_citation: Guarnaccia, P.J. et al., J Nerv Ment Dis, 1993; DSM-5 Glossary of Cultural Concepts of Distress.

entries.cultural_expression: con meo ngoi tren nguc toi
entries.literal_translation: a cat is sitting on my chest
entries.clinical_mapping: Common Vietnamese metaphor for chest pressure or tightness. In elderly patients, strongly suggests angina pectoris or cardiac-origin chest pain. Do not dismiss as anxiety without cardiac workup.
entries.source_citation: Nguyen, D. & Beland, F., J Gen Intern Med, 2011; Yeo, S., Western J Nursing Research, 2004.

entries.literal_translation: hit by the wind / wind attack
entries.clinical_mapping: Traditional Vietnamese belief that wind entering the body causes illness. Symptoms include sudden headache, dizziness, neck stiffness, facial paralysis, or stroke-like symptoms. Must urgently evaluate for CVA/TIA.
entries.source_citation: Pham & McPhee, West J Med, 1992; Groleau & Kirmayer, Transcultural Psychiatry, 2004.

entries.literal_translation: heat inside the body / internal heat
entries.clinical_mapping: Vietnamese hot-cold imbalance concept. Internal heat manifests as mouth sores, skin rashes, constipation, dark urine, irritability. Patient may refuse 'hot' foods or medications. Evaluate for dehydration, UTI, dermatitis, or oral pathology.
entries.source_citation: Nguyen, M.D., Vietnamese Americans, in Cross-Cultural Medicine, Grossman & Cardoso, 2006.

entries.clinical_mapping: In Vietnamese women, often a euphemism for gynecological complaints including menstrual pain, pelvic inflammatory disease, or reproductive concerns. Patients may be reluctant to elaborate without a same-gender provider.
recommended_screenings: pelvic exam (with female provider if possible)
entries.source_citation: Do, M., J Immigr Minor Health, 2009; Tran, J.H., Asian Am Pac Isl J Health, 2001.

entries.clinical_mapping: Dhat syndrome: culture-bound condition primarily in South Asian men. Patient believes loss of semen (through nocturnal emission, urination, or masturbation) causes fatigue, weakness, anxiety, and sexual dysfunction. Maps clinically to somatic symptom disorder, depression, or sexual dysfunction.
entries.source_citation: Sumathipala et al., Br J Psychiatry, 2004; DSM-5 Cultural Concepts of Distress; Bhatia & Malik, Br J Psychiatry, 1991.

entries.clinical_mapping: Ayurvedic hot-cold concept similar to Vietnamese and Chinese systems. Increased body heat associated with anger, skin rashes, acid reflux, mouth ulcers, and urinary symptoms. Patient may refuse 'heating' foods (meat, spices) or medications.
recommended_screenings: medication review (patient may be non-compliant with medications perceived as 'heating')
entries.source_citation: Quah, S.R., International Encyclopedia of Public Health, 2008; Nichter, M., Anthropology & Medicine, 1981.

entries.clinical_mapping: Belief that jealousy or admiration causes illness, especially in children and pregnant women. Symptoms include unexplained fever, fussiness in infants, sudden illness after social event. Clinically evaluate for infectious causes, dehydration.
entries.source_citation: Maloney, C. (1976). The Evil Eye. Columbia University Press; Durkin-Longley, Soc Sci Med, 1984.

entries.clinical_mapping: Vivid metaphor for severe epigastric burning. Strongly suggests peptic ulcer disease, GERD, or gastritis. In South Asian patients, also consider H. pylori infection (higher prevalence in this population).
entries.source_citation: Graham et al., Dig Dis Sci, 1991; Miwa et al., J Gastroenterol Hepatol, 2008.

entries.clinical_mapping: Traditional Chinese Medicine concept. Patient reports fatigue, shortness of breath, weak voice, poor appetite, spontaneous sweating. Clinically evaluate for anemia, hypothyroidism, heart failure, chronic fatigue, or depression.
entries.source_citation: Kaptchuk, T.J. (2000). The Web That Has No Weaver. McGraw-Hill; Cheng, X., Chinese Acupuncture and Moxibustion, 2010.

entries.clinical_mapping: Chinese hot-cold imbalance. Internal fire causes sore throat, mouth ulcers, acne, constipation, nosebleeds, irritability, insomnia. Patient may self-treat with cooling herbs. Evaluate for infection, hypertension, or anxiety.
recommended_screenings: ask about herbal supplement use (potential drug interactions)
entries.source_citation: Kleinman, A. (1980). Patients and Healers in the Context of Culture. University of California Press.

entries.clinical_mapping: Ambiguous expression that can mean emotional distress OR physical cardiac symptoms. Chinese patients may use 'heart' to describe both emotional pain and chest pain. ALWAYS rule out cardiac causes before assuming psychological origin.
recommended_screenings: then PHQ-9 if cardiac workup negative
clarify: physical vs emotional with interpreter
entries.source_citation: Kleinman, A. (1986). Social Origins of Distress and Disease. Yale University Press; Ryder et al., J Abnorm Psychol, 2008.

entries.literal_translation: exposure to hot-cold imbalance causing tremors
entries.clinical_mapping: Filipino belief that sudden temperature change (e.g., washing hands after ironing, exposure to cold after sweating) causes hand tremors, joint pain, and weakness. Clinically evaluate for essential tremor, carpal tunnel, electrolyte imbalance, or thyroid dysfunction.
entries.source_citation: Tan, M.L. (2008). Revisiting Usog, Pasma, Kulam. UP Press; Anderson, J.N., Soc Sci Med, 1983.

entries.literal_translation: illness caused by stranger's greeting or gaze on an infant
entries.clinical_mapping: Filipino belief that a stranger's attention can cause illness in babies. Infant presents with fever, excessive crying, vomiting, and diarrhea after being greeted by an unfamiliar person. Evaluate for febrile illness, viral gastroenteritis, or dehydration.
entries.source_citation: Tan, M.L. (2008). Revisiting Usog, Pasma, Kulam. UP Press; Jocano, F.L., Philippine World-View, 1969.

entries.clinical_mapping: Filipino hot-cold concept. High body heat linked to urinary tract infections, skin eruptions, and irritability. Patient may refuse medications perceived as 'heating.' Similar concept to Vietnamese and Chinese internal heat.
entries.source_citation: Sanchez & Gaw, Psychiatric Services, 2007; Edman & Johnson, Cult Divers Ment Health, 1999.

entries.clinical_mapping: Korean culture-bound syndrome caused by suppressed anger over time. Symptoms: chest tightness, heat sensation, headache, insomnia, palpitations, epigastric mass sensation. Maps to somatization disorder, depression with somatic features, or anxiety.
recommended_screenings: cardiac workup to rule out organic cause
psychosocial assessment focusing on family conflict or grief
entries.source_citation: Min, S.K., Psychiatry Investigation, 2009; Lin, K.M. et al., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1992; DSM-5 Cultural Concepts of Distress.

entries.clinical_mapping: Korean idiom of distress describing a weighted-down, lethargic feeling. Can indicate depression (psychomotor retardation), hypothyroidism, chronic fatigue syndrome, or heart failure. Korean patients may express depression through bodily heaviness rather than mood words.
entries.source_citation: Pang, K.Y., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1998; Kim et al., J Korean Med Sci, 2005.

entries.clinical_mapping: In Arabic, 'qalb' (heart) is used for both physical cardiac pain and emotional distress. Must distinguish between physical chest pain and emotional suffering. Always perform cardiac workup first, especially in older patients or those with risk factors.
entries.source_citation: Hamdan, A., Transcultural Psychiatry, 2009; Al-Krenawi & Graham, Int J Soc Psychiatry, 2000.

entries.clinical_mapping: Widespread belief across Arab cultures that envy causes illness, especially sudden unexplained symptoms after social events. Patients may attribute genuine medical conditions to spiritual causes. Important: validate the patient's framework while ensuring medical evaluation.
recommended_screenings: comprehensive exam based on presenting symptoms
do not dismiss belief but ensure organic causes ruled out
entries.source_citation: Rassool, G.H. (2015). Cultural Competence in Caring for Muslim Patients. Palgrave Macmillan.

entries.clinical_mapping: Some Arab patients may attribute seizures, dissociative episodes, or psychotic symptoms to jinn possession. Clinically essential to evaluate for epilepsy, dissociative disorder, psychosis, or medication non-compliance. Respectful acknowledgment of belief system while pursuing medical workup.
entries.source_citation: Dols, M.W. (1992). Majnun: The Madman in Medieval Islamic Society. Clarendon Press; Al-Habeeb, T.A., Ann Saudi Med, 2003.

entries.clinical_mapping: Persian idiom of distress. Like Arabic and Chinese, 'heart' in Farsi encompasses both physical and emotional domains. Can mean chest pain, palpitations, anxiety, sadness, or worry. Always rule out cardiac etiology first.
recommended_screenings: clarify physical vs emotional with interpreter
entries.source_citation: Good, B.J. (1977). The heart of what's the matter. Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1(1); Barkow, J., Iranian Studies, 1975.

entries.clinical_mapping: Persian expression for generalized emotional distress, similar to Spanish 'nervios.' Encompasses anxiety, irritability, insomnia, somatic complaints, and difficulty concentrating. May indicate GAD, depression, adjustment disorder, or chronic stress.
entries.source_citation: Good, B.J. et al., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1985; Behrouzan, O., Prozak Diaries, Stanford University Press, 2016.

entries.literal_translation: the spirit catches you and you fall down
entries.clinical_mapping: Hmong term for epilepsy. The condition is seen as both a curse and a spiritual gift. Families may resist anti-epileptic medication because seizures are viewed as a connection to the spiritual world. Critical for medication compliance discussions.
recommended_screenings: family meeting with culturally competent interpreter
entries.source_citation: Fadiman, A. (1997). The Spirit Catches You and You Fall Down. Farrar, Straus and Giroux. (Landmark text in cross-cultural medicine.)

entries.clinical_mapping: Hmong belief that the soul has left the body, often after a fright, accident, or trauma. Symptoms include listlessness, loss of appetite, crying, withdrawal. In children, may present as failure to thrive. Clinically evaluate for depression, PTSD, or organic causes of lethargy.
entries.source_citation: Fadiman, A. (1997). The Spirit Catches You and You Fall Down; Culhane-Pera et al., Healing by Heart: Clinical and Ethical Case Stories, 2003.

entries.clinical_mapping: Somali patients may attribute psychiatric symptoms (hallucinations, disorganized behavior, sudden personality changes) to jinn possession. Essential to rule out psychotic disorders, substance use, and organic brain pathology while respecting the patient's cultural framework.
entries.source_citation: Carroll, J.K., BMC Int Health Hum Rights, 2004; Guerin et al., Australas Psychiatry, 2004.

entries.literal_translation: desperate longing / obsessive desire to leave
entries.clinical_mapping: A condition observed primarily in Somali refugee populations. Characterized by restlessness, inability to concentrate, weight loss, insomnia, and obsessive focus on migration or resettlement. Maps clinically to depression, adjustment disorder, or cultural bereavement.
recommended_screenings: social work consult for resettlement support
entries.source_citation: Horst, C. (2006). Transnational Nomads. Berghahn Books; Rousseau et al., Transcultural Psychiatry, 2001.

entries.literal_translation: heart-related illness / illness of the heart
entries.clinical_mapping: Ethiopian expression where 'heart' encompasses emotional and physical distress. Can indicate chest pain, palpitations, grief, anxiety, or a broken heart from loss. Dual evaluation for cardiac and psychiatric causes essential.
entries.source_citation: Alem et al., Ethiopian Medical Journal, 1999; Jacobsson, L., Acta Psychiatr Scand, 2002.

entries.clinical_mapping: Brazilian folk illness. Patient feels their chest is 'open' or split, causing chest pain, cough, and respiratory difficulty. Often self-treated with binding the chest. Clinically evaluate for costochondritis, pneumonia, GERD, or anxiety.
entries.source_citation: Nations, M.K. & Rebhun, L.A., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1988; Helman, C.G., Culture, Health and Illness, 2007.

entries.clinical_mapping: Latin American folk illness in infants. Parents believe the fontanelle has 'fallen' causing the baby to have diarrhea, fever, and inability to feed. The sunken fontanelle is actually a clinical sign of severe dehydration. URGENT: assess and treat dehydration immediately.
entries.source_citation: Trotter, R.T., Medical Anthropology, 1985; Pachter, L.M., Arch Pediatr Adolesc Med, 1994. NOTE: This is a medical emergency indicator. The folk illness label should not delay treatment.

entries.clinical_mapping: TCM concept. Patient reports fatigue, lower back pain, sexual dysfunction, frequent urination, hair loss, and poor memory. In TCM, kidneys govern vitality and reproductive function. Clinically evaluate for chronic kidney disease, diabetes, testosterone deficiency, or depression.
entries.source_citation: Kaptchuk, T.J. (2000). The Web That Has No Weaver; Ots, T., Culture, Medicine and Psychiatry, 1990.
